data.title: 2 Hong Kong men arrested over vandalising Legco election campaign posters
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested two men for allegedly vandalising campaign materials for the coming Legislative Council election.
Police said on Saturday that officers found three vandalised election campaign posters hanging from the Tong Mi Road footbridge in Mong Kok on Thursday.
After an investigation, officers arrested two local men, aged 29 and 34, in Mong Kok on Friday on suspicion of criminal damage, the force said.
Police said the two suspects were being detained as part of the investigation.
“Police reiterate that the force will adopt a zero-tolerance attitude towards any attempt or intention to interfere with or disrupt the Legislative Council election, and make every effort to conduct investigations,” Inspector Yip Ho-lam said.
“Police will also do their utmost to enforce the law strictly and work closely with the public to ensure that residents can vote safely in the Legislative Council election on December 7.”
It is an offence for a person who destroys or damages any property belonging to another without lawful excuse, and carries a maximum penalty of 10 years in prison.
Police conducted multiple law enforcement actions in recent weeks to crack down on vandalism of election campaign materials.
Three men were arrested last weekend as part of separate cases for allegedly damaging election campaign banners.
On Friday, the Independent Commission Against Corruption also arrested two men and a woman for allegedly reposting online content inciting others to abstain from voting or to cast invalid ballots in the election.
The coming election will see 51 candidates competing for the 20 seats in the 10 geographical constituencies where residents will cast their ballots.
Another 60 are vying for 30 seats in the functional constituencies, and the remaining 50 for 40 seats in the Election Committee constituency. These seats are decided by sector-specific voters.

data.title: Hong Kong’s Cade Wright delivers coming-of-age National Games performance
data.description:
Cade Wright said she “loved every second” of her unexpected challenge for the National Games podium, after the Hongkonger narrowly missed out on a medal in an attritional women’s triathlon on Central Harbourfront.
The 22-year-old Wright finished fourth and delivered what she called an “outstanding, career-best swim” on the way to a time of two hours, one minute and 25 seconds, a shade more than two minutes behind winner Huang Anqi.
She acknowledged she had not been expecting to feature at the front, given she had one eye on Sunday’s mixed relay.
“I was aiming for top 10 … I’d spoken with my coach [Andrew Wright] about keeping something in reserve, because the relay is where we have medal potential,” she said.
“But it was very exciting. I was loving every second of being in medal contention.”

She entered the closing run leg in a small pack hunting down long-time leader Lin Xinyu, who would claim silver.
“My run form’s been improving a lot, but some of the girls had a better day than me,” Wright said. “I’m so chuffed with fourth. I never thought I would be remotely competing.”
Andrew Wright said he did not share his athlete’s surprise.
“One thing lacking in a lot of our athletes is self-belief,” he said. “I can see the talent in these guys.”
The coach said that when Wright completes her degree in mathematics at Edinburgh University “we will see her full talent shining through”.
“Everyone else in that field has been a full-time athlete since a young age,” he said. “Cade still isn’t; she’s in university doing a very hard course.”
Cade Wright said she had gained an appreciation of what was possible.
“We have a great coach and training environment, and so much support, so I hope for better results in major Games,” said Wright, whose parents travelled from Edinburgh to watch her. “I’m going to find them and give them a hug.”
After going in under the radar on Saturday, Wright will compete under medal expectation on Sunday. “Pressure is a privilege,” she said. “There will be pressure, but I really enjoy what I do.”

data.title: 18 injured in double-decker bus crash at stop near Hong Kong airport
data.description:
A double-decker bus crashed at a stop near Hong Kong airport on Saturday, leaving at least 18 people injured.
Police said they had received a report from the driver of a Citybus vehicle at 3.16pm that it had crashed into a planter by a bus stop close to the airport’s Terminal One.
“Eighteen people have been injured, with some sent to Princess Margaret Hospital and others sent to North Lantau Hospital. It is unclear if the driver is among the injured,” a spokesman said.
The spokesman added that the bus stop was at 1 Cheong Hong Road, with the vehicle heading in the direction of the airport.
The bus was part of Citybus’ A12 service, which goes from Siu Sai Wan to the airport.

data.title: Pluribus, AppleTV+’s dystopian sci-fi, makes the end of the world look happier than ever
data.description:
The end of the world is not usually so cheery.
In Pluribus, the new Apple TV+ series from Breaking Bad creator Vince Gilligan, scientists encounter a mysterious repeating signal coming from outer space.
That discovery leads researchers working for the United States military to encode a sort of virus that inevitably gets set loose and, after brief periods of mass seizures, leads to a psychic harmony among every human on Earth, who now operate as an “us”.
The thing is, they are really very nice. They offer pooled resources, true equality and, above all else, contentment.
For Carol Sturka (Rhea Seehorn), a grumpy romantasy writer who is immune and surrounded by smiling altruists at her home in Albuquerque, New Mexico, things could not be worse.

Typically, post-apocalyptic and dystopian speculative fiction narratives offer worst-case scenarios. Think Fallout, 28 Years Later and The Last of Us.
Pluribus, though, forces its viewers to genuinely consider whether giving in to this new world would be so bad after all.
“If this is a vision of absolute happiness in the calmest, most secure way possible, but it means losing being what is distinctively you, is it worth it? That’s obviously the tension that we’re trying to figure out,” Gordon Smith, the show’s executive producer, says.
“And we don’t know.”
Smith says Pluribus – Latin for “of many” – stands apart from other The Body Snatcher-esque projects because it does not have Cold War concerns. Those films released from the 1950s to the 1980s, as well as countless episodes of The Twilight Zone, were energised by fears of nuclear war and communism.
Pluribus feels radical because Carol’s enemies genuinely seem to care. They let animals out of zoos. They provide healthcare for everyone. They are vegetarians and they refuse to kill any living being, even a fly. Is the modern world just too cynical to believe ending it could actually be paradise?
In fact, when Carol, in the second episode, meets with the other English speakers who have not yet “joined” with the others, she is in the minority. One man is charmed that these people will seemingly cater to his every need. Another woman maintains that her son is still very much her child, even though he now shares a hivemind.

Carol is, in a way, the ugly American – loud, abrasive and willing to break up the party with her harsh truths.
“There’s 7 billion people on one side,” Smith says, noting that the writers felt as if they had to make it feel as though there was “some beauty on the other side” so Carol actually has some hard decisions to make.
The series is something of a Rorschach test, Smith says. It is not trying to directly evoke the news cycle, even though some viewers have already regarded it as a show about artificial intelligence.
The joined are a little bit like ChatGPT come to life with their all-consuming knowledge – every person on Earth now has the skills and memories of everyone else – but without the spuriousness of a learned language model. The eeriest thing about them is that you can see hints of the people they were before this all happened, the pain they have pushed down.
“If we’re feeling didactic or like we’ve got a lesson, then we’re steering into the wrong waters,” Smith says. “We want to make sure this is a human scale.”
But at the same time, it is hard to watch Pluribus and not think about the times in which we live, even if the metaphor is not as obvious as it is in, say, The Running Man or The Handmaid’s Tale, where the rise of fascism is on the menu.
These days, it feels as if polarisation is the language of humanity, and not in a good way. One just needs to log on to X, formerly known as Twitter, to see that.
Carol, in some ways, represents our fractured world with her fury. At the same time, we must root for her. That is in part because if she simply gave up, that would not make for very entertaining television.
More crucially, however, it is because our ability to feel is what makes us human, and is not hanging on to humanity what all our dystopias are about, anyway?

data.title: From TV dramas to diplomacy, Bangladesh-Turkey ties are ‘growing stronger’
data.description:
In a recording studio in Dhaka, voice-over artist Rubaiya Matin Gity dubs the latest Turkish soap opera to become a megahit in Bangladesh – a pop culture trend that reflects growing ties between the two countries.
“Yasmeen! Yasmeen! I have fallen in love ...” the 32-year-old actor cried in Bangla, her eyes fixed on the screen playing new episodes of the Turkish drama Kara Sevda, or Endless Love, which has captivated millions of viewers in the South Asian nation.
The success of Turkish shows, challenging the once unrivalled popularity of Indian television dramas, is the sign of a change that extends far beyond Bangladeshi screens.
It mirrors shifting alliances and expanding diplomatic, trade and defence relations between the two Muslim-majority nations, 5,000km (3,000 miles) apart.

More Turkish restaurants are opening in Bangladesh and there is a general interest in learning the language, coupled with rekindled warmth between the two governments, set against increasingly fractious relations between Dhaka and New Delhi.
An interim government has led Bangladesh since an uprising last year toppled the autocratic rule of Sheikh Hasina – who fled to old ally India, where she has resisted extradition, turning relations between the two neighbours icy.
Ties between Ankara and Dhaka have not always been smooth, but they “are growing stronger now”, said Md Anwarul Azim, professor of international relations at the University of Dhaka.
“The relationship faltered twice,” he said, first in 1971 when Bangladesh separated from Pakistan, and then in 2013, when Dhaka hanged men accused of war crimes during the independence struggle.
Bilateral trade remains modest, but according to Azim, Turkey offers Bangladesh an alternative to its reliance on China as its main weapons supplier.
Ankara’s defence industry boss Haluk Gorgun visited Dhaka in July, and Bangladesh’s army chief General Waker-Uz-Zaman is expected in Turkey later this month to discuss production of military equipment.
Bangladesh has also shown interest in Turkish drones, technology Ankara has reportedly supplied to Pakistan, India’s arch-enemy.
Dhaka’s interim leader, Nobel Peace Prize winner Muhammad Yunus, said he was “focused on further deepening” ties with Ankara, after meeting a Turkish parliamentary delegation this month.
“Bangladesh stands ready to work hand in hand with Turkey to unlock new opportunities for our people,” Yunus said.

Alongside formal ties, cultural links are also deepening.
Ezaz Uddin Ahmed, 47, head of programming at the channel that pioneered Turkish dramas in Bangladesh, said that Deepto TV had “a dedicated team of translators, scriptwriters, voice artists and editors” working to meet the growing demand.
Its breakout hit came in 2017 with a historical epic that eclipsed Indian serials and “surpassed all others” in terms of popularity, Ahmed said.
Riding on that success, Deepto TV and other Bangladeshi broadcasters snapped up more Turkish imports – from Ottoman sagas to contemporary family dramas.
Interest in the Turkish language has followed suit, with several leading institutions now offering courses.
“I have 20 students in a single batch,” said Sheikh Abdul Kader, a trainer and economics lecturer at Jagannath University. “There is growing demand.”
For some, the love for all things Turkish doesn’t end there.
Business owner Tahiya Islam, 33, has launched a Turkish-themed clothing line, and inspired by Ottoman traditions, even took up horse riding.
“During the Ottoman era, couples used to go out on horseback,” she said. “Now, my husband rides too – and I even have my own horse.”

data.title: With AI looking increasingly like a liability, a storm is coming
data.description:
A recent Financial Times front-page lead, headlined “Tech stocks suffer $1.2tn AI sell-off”, was followed a few days later by a comment elsewhere that tech stocks were the only cloud over an otherwise sunny Wall Street. Weather forecasters would be ashamed of such a simplistic assertion.
Artificial intelligence and, more generally, tech stocks have become the great overarching gods that dominate the stock market firmament. To suggest they are immortal and the investment sky will remain blue even as they are toppled is naive to the point of being fatuous. It never rains but it pours, to use more mundane language.
And it will rain most heavily on Wall Street where tech and AI fever has reached the point of delirium. The Tokyo stock market, which has benefited from investment flows leaving the United States, will provide a temporary haven. But the typhoon will spread its skirts before long.
That tech stocks shed a cool trillion dollars in the space of a week should be enough to send investors running for cover from an approaching storm, and it will not be simply paper wealth that is destroyed. The negative wealth effect of tumbling stock prices will ramify throughout economic and financial systems.
US$1 trillion may not seem a disaster against the total capitalisation of some 770 tech stocks globally (of which AI chipmaker Nvidia accounted for US$5 trillion until recently). But apart from the sudden speed of the price slide, the AI sector’s profit outlook is worrisome.
It is reminiscent of the situation with IT stocks some 25 years ago when stock price valuations for many companies in what was then the ultra hi-tech information technology sector reached super-high levels, way ahead of profit projections. Some IT companies were actually loss-making but that did not deter fevered investors. The IT bubble consequently collapsed, dragging down most other stocks with it.

Tech stocks now have total revenues of around US$3.5 trillion and their weighted average price-to-earnings ratio is 45. But it is the tech sector’s projected spending, rather than earnings, that points to (potentially big) trouble ahead.
JP Morgan expects AI spending to run into trillions of dollars in the coming years, setting a high bar for the return on investment. The investment bank warned that “to drive a 10 per cent return on our modelled AI investments through 2030 would require ~US$650 billion of annual revenue into perpetuity, which is an astonishingly large number”.
The obvious question this raises is whether AI stock investors are prepared to wait so long for the return on their investment and even to stump up huge amounts of capital to finance the projected mega capital expenditures on new data centres. By the same token, will banks be prepared to lend when the return on investment is so uncertain and far off? Or will they all simply fall out of love with AI and the wider tech sector?
Where could they find equally glamorous (if superficial) attractions? Consumer stocks are unlikely, given how the US middle class is pulling back on spending. Likewise, the unglamorous capital goods sector and the capital-hungry climate change alleviation sector.
The truth is, stock markets tend to focus on the next big thing and the AI sector is looking less like the next big financial opportunity and more like the next big financial liability. Hence the recent move towards the door by more sophisticated investors who have seen the writing on the wall. That could soon morph into a stampede.


The next big thing could be defence stocks as governments in Japan, Europe and elsewhere splurge on defence spending. But while this implies a profit boost for arms makers, it also points to rising fiscal deficits, which are bad news for bond and equity markets alike.
All this suggests the era of high-flying stock prices is coming to an end, not least in the US, where Wall Street excesses tend to dominate global investor sentiment.
That, of course, will not be the end of the story. A stock market crash or slide does not only destroy paper wealth, it also curbs consumer spending and capital investment ability, whether the collapse is in stocks, bonds, real estate or other asset classes. People feel less wealthy when asset prices decline. They spend less and save or hoard more. That in turn translates into less willingness to invest in the future, whether in AI or elsewhere.
The next market crash will almost certainly not be the last. A recent paper from the Bank for International Settlements in Switzerland casts an interesting light on boom-and-bust episodes.
It argues that “the Achilles’ heel of the international monetary and financial system is that it amplifies the ‘excess financial elasticity’ of domestic policy regimes, i.e. it exacerbates their inability to prevent the build-up of financial imbalances, or outsize financial cycles, that lead to serious financial crises and macroeconomic dislocations”.
Will it take another financial crash to motivate a move towards enlightened policies? Past experience with financial booms and busts suggests it will, even as the plunge in tech stock valuations suggests it may be too late to prevent a crash. Sadly, the “cult of equity” still has too many followers, especially in the US.

data.title: ONE Championship: Rodtang v Nong-O title fight cancelled after ‘Ironman’ hospitalised
data.description:
The highly anticipated flyweight Muay Thai world title fight between Rodtang Jitmuangnon and Nong-O Hama has been cancelled after Rodtang informed ONE Championship medical staff that he was unwell and was taken to hospital for treatment and evaluation.
The promotion confirmed the news on Friday afternoon in Tokyo, ending hopes of a blockbuster showdown at ONE 173 on Sunday.
The cancellation came shortly after a difficult weigh-in morning for both men. Nong-O failed hydration testing and was unable to make weight for the contest, meaning he would have been ineligible to win the vacant flyweight Muay Thai world title even if the fight had proceeded.
The 39-year-old had hoped to inspire a younger generation by showing he could still compete at the highest level late in his career, but those plans have been derailed for now.
Rodtang, who has struggled with weight cuts in the past, passed hydration and made weight on his only attempt, but did so with just minutes remaining before his cut-off. Hours later, however, the reigning fan favourite was withdrawn from the event after reporting that he felt unwell.

“Rodtang has informed ONE medical staff that he is unwell. For the athlete’s health and safety, he has been transported to the hospital and is undergoing treatment and evaluation,” the promotion said in a statement. “The ONE flyweight Muay Thai world title fight between Rodtang and Nong-O has been cancelled and will be rescheduled for a future event.”
There were further weigh-in issues elsewhere on the card, with Japanese kickboxing star Rukiya Anpo failing hydration testing ahead of his ONE Championship debut. His opponent, Marat Grigorian, made weight without difficulty, adding a layer of irony after Anpo had told Grigorian during Thursday’s face-off to “make sure you make weight”.
Anpo’s comment referenced Grigorian’s last outing at ONE 172, where he missed weight for his scheduled clash with Kaito Ono and was accused of eating yakiniku during fight week.
Speaking to the Post after the news broke, Grigorian did not hide his irritation.
“He was talking during the face-off and now he misses weight,” Grigorian said. “He was saying to me to make weight and here it is, he is the one not making weight. I am a professional fighter. It happens, it can happen, but he was talking very big and very loud.
“But for me the fight goes on. I don’t care if he is heavy or not. I will fight. This is what the fans want to hear.”

All other fighters made weight, including “main event” competitors Superbon Singha Mawynn and Masaaki Noiri.
Japanese superstar Takeru Segawa also passed without issue ahead of his clash with Denis Puric. Takeru appeared relaxed and confident during media day, saying: “I trained to win and my condition is very good. I will definitely win on Sunday.”
He also promised a highlight for the home crowd, adding: “I will win by knockout and show the backflip again.”
Takeru credited his tight-knit team for keeping him focused. “We always train together and push each other. Our bond is very strong and that strength comes into the fight,” he said.

data.title: Hong Kong Island election hopefuls vow to fight for ethnic minority groups
data.description:
Legislative Council election candidates competing for seats in Hong Kong Island’s constituencies have vowed to fight for more job opportunities for ethnic minority communities and ensure their voices are heard.
The contenders made the promise on Saturday at the first of the day’s government-run forums for the Hong Kong Island West and East constituencies, held for the first time at Hong Kong Park’s Olympic Square.
But organisers put up curtains at the venue’s entrance, preventing anyone without a ticket from watching the debates, while several police officers patrolled outside.
The morning session hosted the five candidates for Hong Kong Island West, a two-seat constituency that has the highest ethnic minority population, with the contenders asked to outline their plans to support the area’s non-Chinese community.
Incumbent lawmaker Chan Hok-fung of the Democratic Alliance for the Betterment and Progress of Hong Kong said that people from ethnic minority backgrounds wanted their cultures and religions to be respected, in addition to more support in the workplace and daily life.
“Some of them told me: ‘We actually understand Cantonese, and we can read some Chinese too. There are some misunderstandings about us.’ What they really need is equal opportunity,” he added.
Another incumbent, Judy Chan Kapui of the New People’s Party, proposed making more space for ethnic minority communities on district councils and committees, boosting civic participation.
The government should also push businesses to hire people from more diverse backgrounds, she added.

Kwok Wai-keung of the Hong Kong Federation of Trade Unions, who was also seeking re-election, stressed the need to improve labour rights protections for workers from ethnic minority backgrounds, as they had emerged as a key labour force in the city.
District councillor Jeremy Young Chit-on of the Liberal Party highlighted his proficiency in English as a strength for supporting ethnic minority groups.
“To be a lawmaker, one must be highly proficient in both Chinese and English, and possess strong persuasive and communication skills to help ethnic minority people resolve their issues,” he said.
District councillor Wong Chau-ping called for increased resources for community centres intended for various communities and to improve their services.
Hong Kong Island West is the only constituency with as many as three incumbents campaigning for two seats.
The forum was among the 39 sessions organised by the government for the Legco election on December 7. One event is being held for each of the city’s 10 geographical, 28 functional and one Election Committee constituencies.
The other events held throughout the week have seen few clashes among candidates.
On Friday, Chief Secretary Eric Chan Kwok-ki encouraged candidates to express opposing views at debates, but urged them to refrain from “tactics such as smearing, mudslinging and back-stabbing”.
But the first of Saturday’s debates was largely peaceful, only flaring up when Young slammed the three sitting lawmakers for describing their re-election bids as being in dire straits.
“Residents should not be pushed to vote; you vote because you believe in a candidate who deserves your ballot,” he said. None of the trio responded to Young’s remarks.
The final forum for geographical constituencies will be held on Saturday afternoon, covering Hong Kong Island East. The remaining events will cover the functional and Election Committee constituencies.
A total of 161 candidates are competing for the 90 seats in the election.
A total of 51 in the 10 geographical constituencies are vying for 20 seats, and 60 in the 28 functional constituencies for 30 seats. The remaining 50 candidates will contest the 40 seats in the Election Committee constituency.
Residents vote for their representatives in the geographical constituencies, while lawmakers in the functional and the Election Committee constituencies are chosen by small circles of voters within their respective sectors.
Click here for the full list of election candidates.

data.title: EMDR therapy helped Miley Cyrus and Prince Harry, but how does bilateral stimulation work?
data.description:
A therapy to address trauma using guided eye movements has been endorsed by celebrities such as Prince Harry, Miley Cyrus and Sandra Bullock.
Yet when my daughter Hattie told me her therapist had suggested EMDR – eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing – as part of a plan to manage the intrusive thoughts that frequently accompany her sometimes disabling obsessive-compulsive disorder, I was sceptical.
How on Earth, I questioned, could “eye desensitisation” get rid of distressing thoughts that had apparently resisted CBT – cognitive behaviour therapy – and medication?
EMDR’s history
EMDR therapy was developed by Dr Francine Shapiro, an American psychologist and educator, almost 40 years ago.
The discovery in 1987 was accidental. Walking in a park one day, she noticed that moving her eyes from side to side seemed to reduce the distress associated with upsetting memories.

She hypothesised that eye movements had a desensitising effect on negative emotions and began testing this on other people. She subsequently developed the method known as EMDR today.
How bilateral stimulation helps
While revisiting a negative memory, the patient’s eyes track the therapist’s moving finger or a light from side to side, which creates bilateral stimulation.
This sparks communication between the left and right hemispheres of the brain, a process similar to REM (rapid eye movement) sleep, crucial for learning, memory consolidation and emotional processing. It can then properly file away and integrate traumatic memories, lessening their emotional intensity.
Other forms of bilateral stimulation include tapping, listening to alternating tones or clicks in each ear or holding small devices that vibrate gently and alternate between the left and right hands.
Dr Julia Alisha Andre, a Hong Kong-based trauma specialist, clinical psychologist, certified EMDR therapist and ardent advocate of EMDR, says “it treats the root, not just the symptoms”.


Changing views
“Despite research already emerging in the 1990s, even 10 years ago many clinicians still viewed it with scepticism and sometimes dismissed it as a ‘voodoo’ technique,” Andre says.
Today, it is backed by the World Health Organization and other prominent health bodies.
“People now recognise EMDR as an evidence-based therapy and many seek it out specifically.”
Singer Miley Cyrus claims the therapy “saved her life” after she developed paralysing stage fright and panic attacks; actress Sandra Bullock says it helped her heal from PTSD and trauma after a 2014 home invasion; and Prince Harry says he was able to process trauma and unresolved anxiety related to the death of his mother, Princess Diana.
As Andre observes, our past changes us profoundly – and often in ways we do not appreciate; increasingly, people are aware of how a body “keeps the score”.
“Our bodies and the more primitive parts of our brains hold experiences that are not easily accessed through reasoning or language, yet they influence how we feel and function every day,” Andre says, referring to the ideas neuroscientist Bessel van der Kolk presented in his bestselling book, The Body Keeps the Score.
The experience of an EMDR session

EMDR feels different from traditional talk therapy. “It is more experiential and body-based, though still guided, within a structured framework,” Andre says.
“We usually begin by identifying a specific memory, image, thought or body sensation that feels distressing or stuck. The client is asked to notice what comes up as images, emotions, beliefs or sensations, while following a form of bilateral stimulation.”
Because many of Hattie’s sessions are virtual, she does “tapping” – she crosses her arms and taps opposite shoulders.
“First, we go through the negative EMDR cognitions,” she says, referring to a list of negative beliefs such as, “I am worthless; I am a bad person.”
“The aim is to get to the positive side of the list, ‘I am worthy, I am lovable,’ et cetera.”

Hattie’s therapist asks her to identify the distressing thought or conjure the upsetting image, which could be of “somebody being hurt – because of me, an accident I’ve caused”, she says.
Many of Hattie’s resistant thoughts and worries are associated with false memories, which are common in OCD. They are not real, but her OCD persuades her that they are.
“I find the first part of my sessions very hard,” Hattie says, confronting awful anxieties that have gnawed away at her, sometimes for weeks. But her therapist keeps checking in with her.
This is important, as Andre observes, “to help the client stay grounded and ensure the process remains safe and tolerable”.
Reprocessing memories lessens their intensity
Considering the anxiety levels at the start of a session, Hattie is always surprised at how quickly the negative feelings start to dissipate.
During stimulation sets, the brain begins to process the memory or experience, Andre says.
“We do not re-experience the trauma, but rather reprocess it. The goal is not to erase memories, but to change how they are stored and felt so they become part of the past rather than something that keeps intruding in the present.”
By the end of a session, Andre says, clients describe a sense of relief and feel calmer.
Hattie agrees.

“EMDR seems to unstick certain worries – even those I have had for months, years, things I can’t get over that keep resurfacing,” Hattie says. “And it keeps ‘unsticking’ them; the anxiety continues to lessen in the days after a session.”
Over time, Andre says, “the same triggers that once brought distress begin to feel neutral, as the nervous system learns that the danger has truly passed”.
EMDR treats many conditions
EMDR was originally developed for post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and has since been shown to be effective across a wide range of conditions where unprocessed or overwhelming experiences play a role.
Andre also uses EMDR to treat “anxiety disorders, panic attacks, phobias, depression, grief, obsessive thoughts, relationship difficulties and chronic shame”.

The therapy can be less costly and can show results more quickly than other forms of therapy. According to the Association for Psychological Science, EMDR is typically delivered one or two times a week for a total of six to 12 sessions, although some people benefit from fewer sessions. Sessions can also be conducted on consecutive days.
Andre says the number of sessions required depends on the nature of what is being treated.
“For some, it is a short-term, focused intervention targeting a specific event. In these cases, just a few sessions can bring significant relief.”
For others, those with complex trauma, long-standing anxiety, or obsessive-compulsive symptoms, EMDR is most effective as part of a longer-term process.

Hattie experienced her first EMDR session five years ago and now has it only when she has a spike in symptoms.
OCD patients respond well
There are good reasons EMDR can be truly helpful in Hattie’s case.
Beneath OCD, explains Andre, “there lies a dysregulated autonomic nervous system that is not fully accessible through reasoning alone”. I know this – I have tried hard to explain to Hattie the baselessness of her anxieties, but that seems to feed her anxiety.
You cannot simply think or behave your way out of OCD thinking, Andre says. CBT is highly evidence-based, she says, but “I often see it as more of a plaster approach, helpful for managing symptoms, but not always addressing the deeper wound.”
EMDR, by contrast, works with the body and nervous system to lower the stress response and reprocess what has been stuck, not just behaviours.
OCD often comes with intrusive images, thoughts, memories, false memories, feelings of guilt, disgust and shame – or catastrophising, as in “What if X happens?”
“EMDR works directly with these memory networks so they lose their emotional charge and power,” she says, helping to defuse the need to perform the compulsions or rituals associated with OCD.
Says Andre, “EMDR helps to truly clean the wound, not just cover it.”
Unpleasant but effective experience
It can be used as an ongoing therapy or in crisis. Hattie has used it in crisis, but has developed a healthy habit to use it to deal with any persistent anxiety or memory before it blooms as critical.
Hattie describes EMDR as a deeply unpleasant experience – “in terms of how it makes you feel, because you have to focus on what’s making you so distressed”. But she also finds it one of the best: “I can’t quite believe it works, but it does.”
“It speaks to both the science of the brain and the truth of the body, and when those two align, real healing can happen,” Andre says.
Like what you read? Follow SCMP Lifestyle on Facebook, X and Instagram. You can also sign up for our eNewsletter here.

data.title: China school slammed for making pupils salute teachers arriving in cars on cold mornings
data.description:
A viral video of primary school pupils in eastern China saluting teachers on cold mornings has sparked a storm of outrage online.
The incident happened at Jianqiao Primary School in Hangzhou, Zhejiang province, a century-old institution known for its status as a key local school and a model of civility.
In the clip, four pupils wearing patrol armbands stand at attention at the school gate.
Each time a teacher’s car drives in, they salute and shout “good morning”. In less than a minute, they greet 10 cars.

The particular salute is known as a symbol of the Young Pioneers, a children’s organisation led by the Chinese Communist Youth League.
While some teachers wave at the pupils, most cars drive past with their windows up.
Meanwhile, another pupil is seen standing in the corridor, checking the appearance of classmates.
The video has attracted more than 100,000 likes on mainland social media, with reactions divided.
Supporters defend the practice as “etiquette education”, arguing it will help instil respect in the children.

However, critics said it was “ridiculous formalism”, warning that it could teach children to equate respect with blind obedience rather than equality.
One online observer said: “Why do the teachers not acknowledge the children’s greetings? With so many kids walking to school, the heavy traffic at the gate is also dangerous.”
A member of the school’s staff told the mainland media outlet Haibao News that the saluting was voluntary, adding that online criticism had affected the children’s enthusiasm.
The school also sees pupils carry out patrol duties during which they check campus safety, etiquette behaviour and lunch management.
Some parents, however, were not pleased.
One reportedly filed a complaint with the local Education Bureau, saying that their child had to wake up 30 minutes earlier each day to make it to school by 7.40am.
“It is a struggle just to stand in the cold and salute the teachers’ cars. It would be better to let the kids sleep an extra ten minutes,” one said.

Another parent posted a screenshot from a class group chat, showing that the school had instructed students to greet teachers at the gate.
On November 6, the local Education Bureau issued a statement, severely criticising the school’s headmaster and ordering corrective action.
Reports indicated that the school would install speed bumps and barriers at the gate and revise its campus etiquette education.
Unconventional practices like this are not rare in China.
In October, a secondary school in central China’s Henan province held a bizarre “guilt exercise”, in which parents knelt to form a human bridge over which pupils walked.
